provenance.source_corpus: The Role of the Forest Soil Propagule Bank in Assisted Natural Recovery after Oil Sands Mining

source_spans.text: LFH treatments had significantly higher species richness (49, 47, 24, and 25 species for LFH 10 cm, LFH 20 cm, peat 10 cm, and peat 20 cm treatments, respectively), plant abundance, and soil nutrients than peat treatments.

LFH treatments had significantly higher species richness (49, 47, 24, and 25 species for LFH 10 cm, LFH 20 cm, peat 10 cm, and peat 20 cm treatments, respectively), plant abundance, and soil nutrients than peat treatments.

LFH treatments had significantly higher species richness (49, 47, 24, and 25 species for LFH 10 cm, LFH 20 cm, peat 10 cm, and peat 20 cm treatments, respectively), plant abundance, and soil nutrients than peat treatments.

LFH treatments had significantly higher species richness (49, 47, 24, and 25 species for LFH 10 cm, LFH 20 cm, peat 10 cm, and peat 20 cm treatments, respectively), plant abundance, and soil nutrients than peat treatments.
LFH treatments had narrower C:N ratios and higher soluble potassium and available phosphorus than peat.
LFH treatments had narrower C:N ratios and higher soluble potassium and available phosphorus than peat.
LFH treatments had narrower C:N ratios and higher soluble potassium and available phosphorus than peat.
20 cm of LFH was more beneficial for plant community establishment than 10 cm.
20 cm of LFH was more beneficial for plant community establishment than 10 cm.
Applying 10 versus 20 cm of donor material increased admixing of fine-textured subsoil, reducing organic carbon, nitrogen, and potassium
Applying 10 versus 20 cm of donor material increased admixing of fine-textured subsoil, reducing organic carbon, nitrogen, and potassium

Applying 10 versus 20 cm of donor material increased admixing of fine-textured subsoil, reducing organic carbon, nitrogen, and potassium
LFH donor material had significantly more plant species emerge (37) from the propagule bank than did peat donor material (19).
LFH donor material had significantly more plant species emerge (37) from the propagule bank than did peat donor material (19).
When applying donor soils, admixing with subsoil materials should be avoided, as it dilutes soil propagules, organic matter, and nutrients.
